sectionName: Psychological, Social & Biological Foundations of Behavior

questions.passage: Researchers studied asthma management in three neighborhoods served by the same public hospital. All participants had physician-diagnosed asthma and the same controller inhaler. Neighborhood A had low median income, crowding, and a 52-minute average commute; B had moderate income and stable housing; C had higher income and nearby pharmacies. Controller refill rates over 6 months were 41%, 63%, and 78%, respectively. Emergency department visits per 100 participants were 29, 17, and 9. Interviews linked missed refills to transportation, shift work, and housing instability rather than disagreement with medical advice.
questions.stem: Which interpretation best fits the asthma results?

explanations.A: Correct. The neighborhood pattern links refill rates and ED use to transportation, work, housing, and pharmacy access, which are nonmedical social conditions shaping health. This answer uses the passage data rather than overextending beyond the study design.
explanations.B: This would attribute behavior mainly to biology. The passage emphasizes social context, expectations, resources, or institutions rather than fixed biological causation. The passage focus is structural barriers to asthma care, so this option is less directly supported.
explanations.C: Operant conditioning changes behavior through reinforcement or punishment. It is not the best fit when the stem asks about group pressure, appraisal, validity, or social structure. The passage focus is structural barriers to asthma care, so this option is less directly supported.
explanations.D: Absolute threshold is the minimum stimulus detected about 50% of the time. The question is not about minimum sensory intensity. The passage focus is structural barriers to asthma care, so this option is less directly supported.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Controller refill rates and emergency department visits per 100 participants across the three neighborhoods in the asthma-management study.

questions.stem: The interview finding about transportation and shift work most directly supports which concept?

explanations.A: A fixed-ratio schedule rewards behavior after a set number of responses. The passage does not describe a predictable response-count reward rule. The passage focus is barriers to asthma care, so this option is less directly supported.
explanations.B: Correct. The missed refills are explained by external constraints that make adherence difficult even when patients accept the prescription. This answer uses the passage data rather than overextending beyond the study design.
explanations.C: Primary appraisal is the first judgment of whether an event is threatening, harmful, challenging, or benign. It is not a measure of social resources or institutions. The passage focus is barriers to asthma care, so this option is less directly supported.
explanations.D: Semantic memory stores facts and meanings. The stem does not ask about general knowledge. The passage focus is barriers to asthma care, so this option is less directly supported.

questions.stem: Why should causal wording be limited in the neighborhood comparison?

choices.C: The study was observational rather than randomly assigned.

explanations.A: Random assignment allocates participants to conditions by chance. The stem may ask about measurement or observational limitations rather than an experimental solution. The passage focus is observational health disparities, so this option is less directly supported.
explanations.B: Continuous reinforcement rewards every target response. The described pattern is not a reward after every response. The passage focus is observational health disparities, so this option is less directly supported.
explanations.C: Correct. Participants were not assigned to income, commute, housing, or pharmacy conditions, so correlated neighborhood factors could contribute to the outcome. This answer uses the passage data rather than overextending beyond the study design.
explanations.D: The hippocampus is important for new explicit memory formation. The stem does not ask about declarative memory neurobiology. The passage focus is observational health disparities, so this option is less directly supported.

questions.stem: Which intervention most directly targets the mechanism described in Neighborhood A?

choices.D: Evening pharmacy delivery for patients with transportation barriers

explanations.A: Classical conditioning pairs stimuli so a neutral cue comes to elicit a response. The passage item is about social, cognitive, or operant processes rather than stimulus pairing. The passage focus is structural access intervention, so this option is less directly supported.
explanations.B: Role strain is difficulty meeting expectations within one role. The stem is not limited to competing demands inside a single role. The passage focus is structural access intervention, so this option is less directly supported.
explanations.C: Sensory adaptation is reduced receptor responsiveness to constant stimulation. The item centers on cognition, social context, or decision processes instead. The passage focus is structural access intervention, so this option is less directly supported.
explanations.D: Correct. Delivery addresses commute and schedule constraints that the interviews identified as reasons for missed refills. This answer uses the passage data rather than overextending beyond the study design.

questions.stem: Which finding would best strengthen the transportation explanation?

choices.A: Within each neighborhood, unreliable transportation predicts lower refill rates.

explanations.A: Correct. This would test the proposed mechanism at the individual level while holding broad neighborhood context more constant. This answer uses the passage data rather than overextending beyond the study design.
explanations.B: Social reproduction is the transmission of inequality across generations. The stem does not trace class advantage across generations. The passage focus is transportation as a mechanism, so this option is less directly supported.
explanations.C: Cannon-Bard theory proposes simultaneous emotion and arousal. It does not emphasize misattribution or labeling of arousal. The passage focus is transportation as a mechanism, so this option is less directly supported.
explanations.D: Positive symptoms add experiences such as hallucinations or delusions. The stem does not describe an added psychotic experience. The passage focus is transportation as a mechanism, so this option is less directly supported.

questions.passage: A statistics course tested a 10-minute values-affirmation essay. Students wrote either about important personal values or about a neutral campus topic before the first exam. Continuing-generation students scored 82% after affirmation and 81% after neutral writing. First-generation students scored 78% after affirmation and 70% after neutral writing. Interviews showed that several first-generation students feared poor performance would confirm assumptions about their background.
questions.stem: The first-generation students’ worry about confirming assumptions illustrates:

explanations.A: Self-serving bias attributes success internally and failure externally. The stem does not describe attribution of personal outcomes in that way. The passage focus is identity-based evaluative threat, so this option is less directly supported.
explanations.B: Correct. They feared that poor exam performance would confirm a negative belief about their social group, which can impair performance under evaluation. This answer uses the passage data rather than overextending beyond the study design.
explanations.C: Mere exposure is increased liking after repeated exposure. The student worry is about confirming a negative group stereotype during evaluation. The relevant evidence instead centers on identity-based evaluative threat.
explanations.D: Social loafing is reduced effort when individual contributions are not identifiable in a group task. The stem is not about anonymous group productivity. The passage focus is identity-based evaluative threat, so this option is less directly supported.

questions.stem: Which comparison best tests whether affirmation was especially associated with first-generation performance?

choices.C: The affirmation-neutral difference for first-generation students versus the same difference for continuing-generation students

explanations.A: Discriminant validity means weak relation with unrelated measures. The passage does not use an unrelated comparison measure. The passage focus is differential intervention association, so this option is less directly supported.
explanations.B: Difference threshold is the smallest detectable difference between stimuli. The passage item asks about a broader psychological or sociological concept. The passage focus is differential intervention association, so this option is less directly supported.
explanations.C: Correct. The key pattern is an interaction: an 8-point difference in one group but a 1-point difference in the other. This answer uses the passage data rather than overextending beyond the study design.
explanations.D: Role exit means disengaging from a central social role. The passage does not describe leaving such a role. The passage focus is differential intervention association, so this option is less directly supported.

questions.stem: What design feature would best support a causal claim about the essay task?

choices.D: Random assignment to affirmation or neutral writing

explanations.A: Selection bias occurs when group membership or participation is systematically nonrandom. The stem does not describe self-selection or volunteer differences. The passage focus is causal inference for affirmation, so this option is less directly supported.
explanations.B: Medicalization defines nonmedical problems as medical issues. The passage does not describe expanding medical labels onto social life. The passage focus is causal inference for affirmation, so this option is less directly supported.
explanations.C: Population aging is a rising proportion of older adults. The stem is not about age structure driving social needs. The passage focus is causal inference for affirmation, so this option is less directly supported.
explanations.D: Correct. Random assignment would help balance prior ability, motivation, and stress across the two writing conditions. This answer uses the passage data rather than overextending beyond the study design.

questions.stem: If the essay works by increasing confidence in statistics ability, the mechanism is:

explanations.A: Correct. Self-efficacy is belief in one’s ability to succeed at a specific task, such as performing well in statistics. This answer uses the passage data rather than overextending beyond the study design.
explanations.B: Anomie is weakened norms or normlessness. The stem does not describe social norm breakdown. The passage focus is task-specific academic confidence, so this option is less directly supported.
explanations.C: Gate-control theory proposes that nonpainful input can modulate pain signaling. The stem is not about pain modulation. The passage focus is task-specific academic confidence, so this option is less directly supported.
explanations.D: Test-retest reliability is score stability over time. The stem is not about repeated measurement stability. The passage focus is task-specific academic confidence, so this option is less directly supported.

choices.B: Affirmation was associated with a larger score difference among first-generation students.

explanations.A: This would attribute behavior mainly to biology. The passage emphasizes social context, expectations, resources, or institutions rather than fixed biological causation. The passage focus is exam-score interpretation, so this option is less directly supported.
explanations.B: Correct. The data show a larger affirmation-neutral gap for first-generation students but do not prove innate ability differences or total elimination of gaps. This answer uses the passage data rather than overextending beyond the study design.
explanations.C: Group polarization is movement toward more extreme views after group discussion. The passage does not describe attitude extremity after discussion. The passage focus is exam-score interpretation, so this option is less directly supported.
explanations.D: Negative symptoms reduce normal function, such as motivation or social engagement. The stem does not describe diminished function. The passage focus is exam-score interpretation, so this option is less directly supported.
